articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47M reserve fund sits untouched while roads crumble
articles.dek: Three years of budget surpluses have filled county coffers. Commissioners say they're saving for a rainy day. Residents on unpaved roads in Century and Bellview say the storm already hit.
brief: Escambia County's general fund reserves have grown to $47.3 million, up from $28 million in 2023, according to budget documents reviewed by The Flightline.
Meanwhile, the county's road maintenance backlog exceeds $120 million, with 84 miles of unpaved roads still awaiting improvements promised in a 2019 infrastructure plan.
Commissioners cite hurricane preparedness and economic uncertainty; residents in Century and Bellview say they've waited long enough.
articles.body:
The pothole on Jakes Lane in Century is deep enough to swallow a basketball. Teresa Wilkins knows because her grandson tried it last summer.

"He dropped it right in there and we couldn't fish it out," Wilkins said, standing at the edge of her gravel driveway on a Thursday morning. "That hole's been there three years now. Every time it rains, it gets bigger."

Jakes Lane is one of 84 miles of unpaved roads in Escambia County that were supposed to be improved under the county's 2019 infrastructure plan. Seven years later, fewer than 12 miles have been completed. The rest remain on a waiting list that grows longer each budget cycle.

At the same time, the county's general fund reserves have swelled to $47.3 million—nearly double what they were three years ago. The money sits in accounts earning modest interest while roads like Jakes Lane deteriorate and residents like Wilkins wonder when their turn will come.

The numbers

Budget documents obtained by The Flightline show a consistent pattern since fiscal year 2023. Each year, the county has ended with a surplus. Each year, commissioners have voted to transfer the excess into reserves rather than accelerate infrastructure spending.

In FY 2023, the surplus was $6.2 million. In FY 2024, $7.8 million. Last year, $5.1 million. The cumulative effect: a reserve fund that now represents roughly 18% of the county's total general fund budget.

County Administrator Wes Moreno defended the approach during a budget workshop last month.

"We've been through hurricanes. We've been through economic downturns," Moreno said. "Having three to four months of operating expenses in reserve isn't excessive. It's prudent."

The Government Finance Officers Association recommends local governments maintain reserves equal to at least two months of operating expenses. Escambia County's current reserves exceed four months' worth.

A different calculation

In Bellview, on the county's western edge, Darrell Thompson has done his own math.

Thompson lives on Crabtree Church Road, an unpaved stretch that serves about 40 homes. When it rains, the road becomes a mud pit. When it's dry, dust coats everything within 50 yards of the roadway. His wife stopped hanging laundry outside years ago.

"They told us in 2020 we were on the list," Thompson said. "Then COVID hit and everything got pushed back. Then they said supply chain issues. Then they said they needed to reassess priorities."

He pulled out a printout from the county's website—the current road improvement schedule. Crabtree Church Road is listed for "evaluation" in 2028.

"Evaluation," Thompson said. "Not paving. Evaluation. That's eight years after they first said we were a priority."

The county's Public Works Department estimates the full backlog of road improvements would cost approximately $120 million. At current funding levels—roughly $8 million per year from the local option gas tax and general fund allocations—the backlog would take 15 years to clear, assuming no new roads are added to the list.

Commission divided

The five-member Escambia County Commission has not spoken with one voice on the reserve question.

District 3 Commissioner Lumon May, whose district includes Century, has repeatedly pushed for reallocating reserve funds to road projects. In February, he proposed transferring $15 million from reserves to a dedicated road improvement fund. The motion failed 3-2.

"We're hoarding money while people can't get ambulances down their streets," May said after the vote. "At some point, the rainy day argument stops making sense when it's already pouring on half the county."

Commission Chairman Jeff Bergosh, who voted against the transfer, said the county needs to maintain fiscal discipline.

"Nobody's going to bail us out if we spend down our reserves and then get hit with a Category 4 hurricane," Bergosh said in an interview. "We learned that lesson after Ivan. We're not going to unlearn it."

Hurricane Ivan struck Pensacola in 2004, causing widespread damage and straining county resources for years afterward. County officials frequently cite Ivan when defending conservative fiscal policies.

But Ivan was 22 years ago. Residents on unpaved roads say the county has had more than two decades to prepare.

Who waits longest

A Flightline analysis of the county's road improvement database found a clear geographic pattern in who gets paved roads and who doesn't.

Of the 84 miles of unpaved county roads, 67 miles—nearly 80%—are located in Districts 3 and 5, which cover the northern and western portions of the county. These areas have lower median household incomes and higher poverty rates than the county average.

Century, the small town near the Alabama border where Teresa Wilkins lives, has a poverty rate of 34%. The median household income is $24,000. More than half of the town's roads are unpaved or classified as "substandard" by county engineers.

In contrast, Districts 1 and 2—covering Pensacola Beach, Gulf Breeze-adjacent areas and the more affluent central corridor—have virtually no unpaved county roads.

County officials say the disparity reflects historical development patterns, not deliberate policy. Newer subdivisions in growing areas were required to build paved roads as a condition of approval. Older rural areas developed before such requirements existed.

"We're not choosing to ignore anyone," said Public Works Director Joy Jones. "We're working through the list as fast as funding allows."

Faster options

Some county governments facing similar backlogs have found ways to accelerate road improvements without draining reserves entirely.

Baldwin County, Alabama—directly across the state line—issued $50 million in bonds in 2022 specifically for road improvements. The debt is being repaid through a dedicated portion of gas tax revenue. Three years later, the county has completed more than 60 miles of paving.

Santa Rosa County, Escambia's neighbor to the east, established a special taxing district for road improvements in rural areas. Property owners in the district pay a modest annual assessment—typically $50 to $100 per parcel—that funds improvements on their roads. Since 2020, the program has paved 23 miles of previously unpaved roads.

Escambia County has not pursued either approach. Moreno, the county administrator, said bonds would add debt service costs that could strain future budgets. Special taxing districts have faced opposition from property rights advocates who argue they amount to taxation without adequate representation.

"Every option has tradeoffs," Moreno said. "The commission has to weigh those tradeoffs."

Waiting on Jakes Lane

Teresa Wilkins doesn't spend much time thinking about government finance policy. She thinks about getting to work on time—a challenge when rain turns Jakes Lane into a obstacle course. She thinks about her grandsons, who can't ride their bikes on a road that's more rocks than surface. She thinks about the ambulance that got stuck trying to reach a neighbor's house last year.

"They had to walk her out to the main road," Wilkins said. "A 78-year-old woman with chest pains, walking through the mud."

The county's official road improvement schedule shows Jakes Lane listed for "evaluation" in 2029. Actual paving, if approved, would likely not occur until 2031 or 2032.

By then, Escambia County's reserve fund—assuming current trends continue—could exceed $60 million.

"I'm 64 years old," Wilkins said. "I've lived on this road my whole life. My parents lived here. I don't know if I'll see it paved before I die. And they've got all that money just sitting there."

She looked down the road toward the pothole that swallowed her grandson's basketball.

"They say they're waiting for a rainy day. What do they think we've been having out here?"

articles.label: Development & Infrastructure
articles.headline: The last fish house on Pensacola Beach
articles.dek: Joe Patti's grandson runs the only commercial fishing operation left on the island. A proposed marina expansion could push him out by fall.
brief: Tommy Patti operates the last commercial fishing dock on Pensacola Beach, a 40-year holdout against resort development
Escambia County's proposed Quietwater Marina expansion would eliminate his lease by September 2026
The county commission will vote on the marina project at its May 15 meeting
articles.body:
The mullet start running before dawn. Tommy Patti is already on the dock at 4:30 a.m., hosing down coolers and checking ice levels, when the first boat idles up to the splintered wooden pilings. Captain Ray Simmons tosses a line. Patti catches it one-handed without looking up.

"Forty-three years I been doing this," Patti says. "My grandfather built this dock. My father worked it. Now it's just me."

The Patti Fish House sits on a quarter-acre of county-leased waterfront at the eastern edge of the Quietwater Beach Boardwalk, sandwiched between a paddleboard rental outfit and the back parking lot of a condo complex. It is the last commercial fishing operation on Pensacola Beach. By September, it may not exist at all.

A family name, a shrinking footprint

Tommy Patti is 67 years old. He is the grandson of Joe Patti, the Sicilian immigrant who built Pensacola's most famous seafood market on the mainland in 1931. The family name still hangs over the DeVilliers Street institution, though the Pensacola Beach operation has always been smaller, scrappier, less known to tourists.

"Joe Patti's gets the tour buses," Tommy says. "I get the guys who actually fish."

The fish house processes between 800 and 1,200 pounds of Gulf catch on a good day — mostly red snapper, grouper, amberjack and triggerfish during their respective seasons, with mullet and sheepshead filling the gaps. Three boats work out of the dock regularly. Another half-dozen stop by when the bite is right.

Patti buys their catch at market rate, packs it in ice and trucks it to restaurants across the Panhandle. He has no retail storefront. No air conditioning in his cinder block processing shed. No website.

"I'm not trying to be a brand," he says. "I'm trying to move fish."

The marina plan

Escambia County has held the lease on the Quietwater property since 1984, subletting parcels to various commercial tenants. Patti's sublease runs month-to-month, a handshake arrangement that has survived four county administrators and countless board turnovers.

That arrangement appears to be ending.

In February, the county's Parks and Recreation Department unveiled a $14.2 million plan to expand the Quietwater Marina from 42 wet slips to 78, adding fuel service, a dry-stack storage facility and a new harbormaster building. The expansion footprint includes the land currently occupied by Patti Fish House.

"The highest and best use of that waterfront is recreational boating," said County Administrator Wes Moreno at a March 28 public workshop. "We have demand for 200-plus slip reservations. We have one aging commercial tenant."

Patti was not invited to speak at the workshop. He found out about the expansion from a friend who saw it on the county's website.

"Nobody called me," he says. "Forty-three years, and I read about it online like everybody else."

What commercial fishing built

There were once a dozen fish houses on Pensacola Beach. Old photographs in the Pensacola Historical Society archives show the island's sound side lined with working docks in the 1950s and 1960s — ice houses, net-mending sheds, fuel pumps, the whole infrastructure of a fishing economy.

They disappeared one by one. Hurricane Frederic in 1979 wiped out several. Rising property values and tourism development took the rest. The last competitor to Patti Fish House, a small operation near the foot of the Bob Sikes Bridge, closed in 2003 when its owner died and his children sold the lease to a jet ski rental company.

"People forget this was a working beach," says Dr. Brian Rucker, a historian at Pensacola State College who has written extensively about the island's commercial past. "The hotels came later. The condos came later. Fish came first."

Rucker argues that operations like Patti's represent more than nostalgia. Commercial docks, he says, provide working waterfront access that state and federal fishing regulations increasingly require.

"You need somewhere to land the catch," Rucker says. "Push the fish houses out, and you push the fishermen to the mainland. That's more fuel, more time, more cost. Eventually it stops being viable."

The numbers

Escambia County's own economic impact study, completed in January 2026 by the Haas Center at the University of West Florida, estimates the Quietwater Marina expansion would generate $3.1 million in annual economic activity and create 22 full-time jobs.

The study does not mention Patti Fish House. It does not estimate the economic impact of its closure.

Patti says his operation employs three people full-time and two part-time. He estimates he pays commercial fishermen approximately $400,000 annually for their catch. He could not provide documentation for that figure.

"I'm not an economist," he says. "I'm a fish buyer. But I know what I pay out."

County records show Patti's sublease payment is $1,840 per month. The marina expansion's dry-stack facility, according to the Haas Center study, would generate an estimated $287,000 in annual slip rental revenue on the same footprint.

What happens next

The Escambia County Commission will hold a public hearing on the marina expansion at its May 15 meeting. If approved, construction would begin in July. Patti's lease would terminate September 1.

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes Pensacola Beach, said in an interview that he has "real concerns" about eliminating the island's last commercial fishing operation but stopped short of saying he would vote against the project.

"There's a heritage component here that matters," Bergosh said. "But I also have to look at what serves the most residents. That's a hard call."

Commissioner Lumon May did not respond to requests for comment. Commissioners Steven Barry, Mike Kohler and Cheryl Johansen declined to discuss a pending vote.

Patti says he has not been offered relocation assistance or an alternative site. He says he has not retained an attorney.

"What am I going to do, sue the county?" he says. "I'm 67 years old. I don't have that kind of fight in me."

Dawn on the dock

By 6:15 a.m., Captain Simmons has offloaded 380 pounds of red snapper. Patti weighs each fish on a hanging scale, marks the total in a spiral notebook, peels off bills from a roll in his front pocket. The transaction takes four minutes.

Two more boats are waiting.

The sun clears the condo towers to the east. Pelicans circle the dock, waiting for scraps. A family of tourists walks past on the boardwalk, headed for the paddleboard rentals. None of them look at the fish house.

"They don't even know what this is," Patti says, watching them pass. "They think seafood comes from a restaurant. They don't know somebody has to catch it first."

He turns back to the boats.

"Somebody has to bring it in," he says. "Somebody has to be here when they do."

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: NAS Pensacola quietly doubles base housing waitlist as private developer misses maintenance benchmarks
articles.dek: Navy families are waiting 14 months for on-base units. The company managing military housing hasn't met mold remediation targets since 2024.
brief: The waitlist for family housing at NAS Pensacola has grown from 340 families in January 2025 to 687 families as of April 2026
Balfour Beatty Communities, the private company managing base housing, has failed to meet Navy mold remediation benchmarks for seven consecutive quarters
Average wait time for a three-bedroom unit now exceeds 14 months, forcing junior enlisted families into the tight Pensacola rental market
articles.body:
The clipboard on Chief Petty Officer Marcus Webb's kitchen counter holds 23 work orders. Some date back to November. The most recent one, filed three weeks ago, documents black mold spreading behind the drywall in his daughter's bedroom closet.

"They came out, took pictures, said someone would call," Webb said. "That was it."

Webb's family is one of 687 currently on the waitlist for family housing at Naval Air Station Pensacola—a number that has more than doubled since January 2025, according to housing office records obtained through a Freedom of Information Act request. The average wait time for a three-bedroom unit now stretches beyond 14 months.

The housing shortage comes as Balfour Beatty Communities, the private company that has managed military family housing at NAS Pensacola since 2005, continues to miss Navy-mandated maintenance benchmarks. Internal performance reports show the company has failed to meet mold remediation targets for seven consecutive quarters.

A System Under Strain

NAS Pensacola maintains 1,847 family housing units spread across three neighborhoods: Bayou Grande, Corry Village and Chevalier Field. Balfour Beatty operates all three under a 50-year privatization contract signed during the Department of Defense's push to outsource military housing in the early 2000s.

The arrangement was supposed to modernize aging housing stock without requiring direct appropriations from Congress. Private developers would renovate units, collect Basic Allowance for Housing payments from residents and maintain the properties to military standards.

Twenty years later, the model is showing cracks.

Navy performance reviews from 2024 and 2025 document a pattern of delayed maintenance responses and incomplete repairs. Mold remediation—a persistent issue in Northwest Florida's humid climate—has been a particular failure point. Balfour Beatty's contract requires the company to complete mold assessments within 48 hours of a report and remediation within 14 days for confirmed cases.

The company met that standard in only 31% of cases during the first quarter of 2026, according to Navy records. The benchmark is 85%.

Balfour Beatty spokesperson Jennifer Collins said in an email that the company "takes resident concerns seriously" and has "increased staffing and resources dedicated to indoor air quality issues." She declined to address specific performance metrics, citing the proprietary nature of contract negotiations.

Priced Out of Town

For families who can't get base housing, the alternative is the Pensacola rental market—and it's not a cheap one.

The average rent for a three-bedroom apartment in Escambia County hit $1,847 in March, according to Zillow data. That's $234 above the Basic Allowance for Housing rate for an E-5 with dependents stationed at NAS Pensacola.

The gap forces junior enlisted families to make hard choices. Some move to Ferry Pass or Cantonment, trading longer commutes for lower rent. Others squeeze into smaller units or double up with roommates.

Aviation Electronics Technician Second Class Desiree Okonkwo arrived at NAS Pensacola last August with her husband and two children. She put her name on the housing waitlist her first week on base. Nine months later, she's still waiting.

Her family rents a two-bedroom apartment off Navy Boulevard for $1,650 a month. Her BAH rate is $1,613.

"We make it work," Okonkwo said. "But I didn't join the Navy to lose money on rent."

Navy Response

Captain James Thorne, commanding officer of NAS Pensacola, acknowledged the housing situation in a town hall meeting last month but offered few specifics on remedies.

"We're in constant dialogue with our housing partners about improving response times and expanding capacity," Thorne said, according to a recording of the meeting. "I know that doesn't help the family waiting for a phone call that doesn't come. We're working on it."

Navy Region Southeast, which oversees installations from Jacksonville to New Orleans, declined to make officials available for an interview. A spokesperson provided a written statement noting that "housing privatization contracts include performance incentives and penalties" and that the Navy "continues to hold partners accountable to contractual standards."

The statement did not specify what penalties, if any, have been applied to Balfour Beatty.

Oversight Questions

Military housing privatization has drawn increased congressional scrutiny since a 2019 Reuters investigation documented widespread health hazards across bases nationwide. Congress subsequently required the services to establish tenant advocates and implement performance scorecards.

NAS Pensacola's housing office maintains a tenant advocate, but the position has turned over three times since 2023. The current advocate, a civilian Navy employee, started in January and did not respond to requests for comment.

Florida's congressional delegation has largely been silent on the local housing crunch. Offices for Senator Rick Scott, Senator Ashley Moody and Representative Matt Gaetz did not respond to inquiries about whether they had received constituent complaints regarding base housing conditions.

Webb, the chief petty officer with mold in his walls, said he's stopped expecting quick fixes. His family's lease runs through September. He's already looking at apartments in Gulf Breeze.

"Twenty-two years in the Navy," he said. "I've lived in some rough places. But I shouldn't have to fight this hard to get a safe house for my kids on an American base."

The clipboard on his counter keeps growing.

articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Two Escambia nonprofits sue Clerk Pam Childers after she blocks $7,000 in commission-approved payments
articles.dek: First Tee Gulf Coast and the Warrington Emergency Aid Center filed suit Friday after Childers missed a deadline to release funds the Board of County Commissioners unanimously approved. The lawsuit could expose her to personal liability for more than a decade of similar payments.
brief: Two Warrington nonprofits filed a complaint Friday in Escambia Circuit Court after Clerk Pam Childers refused to release $7,000 in discretionary funds the Board of County Commissioners had voted to approve.

The suit, filed by attorney Alex Andrade, seeks a court order compelling payment, a declaratory judgment, and personal liability under Florida Statute 129.09 — which could expose Childers to repayment of more than $1.5 million in similar payments she made from 2016 through 2024.

The Commission unanimously approved $4,500 for First Tee Gulf Coast in early 2025 and $2,500 for the Warrington Emergency Aid Center in October 2025; Childers denied both, citing her belief that county tax dollars cannot fund private organizations.
articles.body:
Two Warrington nonprofits sued Escambia County Clerk of Court Pam Childers on Friday after she refused to release $7,000 in discretionary funds the Board of County Commissioners had voted to pay, setting up a constitutional confrontation over who controls how county money gets spent.

Greater Pensacola Junior Golf Association, doing business as First Tee Gulf Coast, and the Warrington Emergency Aid Center filed the complaint in the Circuit Court of the First Judicial Circuit at 12:26 p.m. The lawsuit — filing number 246771410 — names Childers in her capacity as Clerk of the Circuit Court and Comptroller and seeks a writ of mandamus compelling payment, declaratory relief, and personal liability under Florida Statute § 129.09.

Attorney Alex Andrade of Moore, Hill & Westmoreland, who also serves as a state representative, sent Childers a demand letter April 14 giving her until Friday to release the money or face suit. She did not respond.

The Commission voted to direct $4,500 to First Tee Gulf Coast at three separate meetings — Feb. 6, March 6 and March 25, 2025 — to fund scholarships for at-risk youth to attend golf programming. It approved $2,500 to the Warrington Emergency Aid Center at its Oct. 16, 2025 meeting to reimburse food-pantry expenses. Both organizations submitted required application forms documenting the public purpose of the funds before the Commission voted.

Childers denied both payments. In a March 9, 2026, email to Commissioner Mike Kohler, she wrote that the spending amounted to the county gifting tax revenue to private organizations for purposes unconnected to county programs. She had raised similar concerns at an April 3, 2025, Commission meeting, where she announced she was pausing all discretionary fund payments pending her own review. A memo she sent the Commission that same month stated: the payment will be denied if she does not agree that the mission of county government is being served.

The complaint argues that Childers has no legal authority to make that call. Under Florida Statute § 125.17, the clerk serves as accountant for the Board of County Commissioners, performing a ministerial function rather than a discretionary one. Florida courts have held for more than a century that a clerk may only refuse to sign a warrant when a payment exceeds what the law allows, constitutes an illegal charge, or lacks authorization — conditions the complaint argues do not apply here. The Commission has clear statutory authority under § 125.01 to fund recreational and health-and-welfare programs, and the organizations involved are IRS-designated 501(c)3 public charities.

The suit carries a sharp legal threat for Childers beyond the $7,000 at stake. The same Florida Statute § 129.09 she cites to justify denying payments also imposes strict civil liability on any clerk who signs warrants for unlawful payments. If her constitutional theory is correct — that funding youth golf and a food pantry is an unlawful use of public dollars — then every similar payment she approved from 2016 through 2024 was equally unlawful, and she personally owes the county every dollar. The complaint notes she approved more than $1.5 million in comparable payments over that period, and identifies more than $25,250 in specific payments her own 2025 review flagged as potentially improper.

Marty Stanovich, president and chief executive of First Tee Gulf Coast, said Childers had approved his organization\'s requests each year from 2021 through 2024 through the same process. Janet Plume, president of the Warrington Emergency Aid Center, said the pantry now serves twice as many households as it did at this time last year. A circuit court judge will decide whether the clerk\'s authority extends to overriding a unanimous commission vote — or whether a decade of accepted practice is now unlawful by the Clerk\'s own standard.

articles.label: Education
articles.headline: Escambia school board adopts new student conduct rules for 2026-27, adding drug screens for student drivers
articles.dek: The School Board of Escambia County approved revised elementary and secondary codes of conduct Tuesday. High school students who park on campus must now consent to random drug screening, and blankets, robes and pillows are banned from classrooms.
brief: The School Board of Escambia County voted Tuesday to adopt revised elementary and secondary student codes of conduct for the 2026-27 school year, which will govern the district\'s more than 37,000 students.
High school students who drive and park on campus must consent to random drug screening under the new rules; refusing to submit can result in loss of parking privileges.
A public hearing on the revised codes is scheduled for June 16 at the J.E. Hall Center before final adoption takes effect in August.
articles.body:
The School Board of Escambia County voted Tuesday to adopt revised student codes of conduct that will introduce random drug screenings for student drivers, ban blankets and pillows from classrooms, and update rules on cellphone use and dress standards when more than 37,000 students return in August.

The board approved both the elementary and secondary codes at its regular meeting April 22 at the J.E. Hall Educational Services Center. The changes were developed by a standing committee of students, teachers and principals that meets twice a year to review conduct policies.

The most significant new rule applies to high school students who drive and park on school property. Under the revised secondary code, those students must consent to random drug screening as a condition of holding a parking permit. A student who refuses to submit faces revocation of parking privileges. The policy applies to on-campus parking only.

Other changes span dress policy and classroom behavior. The revised code prohibits students from wearing or bringing blankets, robes or pillows to school. Hemline standards were updated for secondary students. Cellphone usage rules were also revised. Dr. Michael Roberts, the district\'s director of middle school education, described the code as the operational foundation for every school. Board member Tom Harrell, a former principal, said the document reflects the same values it has always carried under an earlier name.

A public hearing on the revised codes is scheduled for 5:30 p.m. June 16 in Room 160 at the J.E. Hall Center, 30 E. Texar Drive. After the hearing, the codes take effect when the 2026-27 school year opens Aug. 12. The revision process runs annually, with students, teachers and principals on the conduct committee recommending changes to the full board each spring.

articles.label: Community
articles.headline: Pensacola plans free hometown concert for Keyla Richardson if she makes American Idol Top 3
articles.dek: Mayor D.C. Reeves and Commissioner Lumon May are coordinating a free event at Community Maritime Park and Wahoos Stadium tentatively set for May 6. Richardson, currently in the Top 7, finds out Monday whether she advances.
brief: The City of Pensacola announced April 23 it is planning a free hometown concert at Community Maritime Park and Wahoos Stadium for May 6, contingent on Keyla Richardson advancing to the American Idol Top 3.
Mayor D.C. Reeves has already had two calls with American Idol producers and is working with Escambia County Commissioner Lumon May on event logistics.
Richardson is currently in the Top 7; the field narrows to five on April 27 and to three on May 4, which would trigger the hometown celebration under the show\'s long-standing tradition.
articles.body:
The City of Pensacola is planning a free hometown concert for American Idol contestant Keyla Richardson at Community Maritime Park and Wahoos Stadium, with Mayor D.C. Reeves already in contact with the show\'s producers and a tentative date of May 6 locked in — if Richardson makes the Top 3.

The city announced the plans April 23 through its official newsroom. Richardson, a Pensacola singer currently among the show\'s Top 7 contestants, faces the next elimination Monday, April 27, when the field narrows from seven to five. The final three are decided May 4. Those contestants travel home for a parade and concert before the season finale, which airs May 11 from Hollywood on ABC.

If Richardson makes it, Pensacola would host an American Idol hometown celebration for the first time. Reeves flew to Hollywood to attend the April 6 episode, bringing signed cards from the community. He has since had two preliminary calls with American Idol producers as the competition narrowed.

Shannon Hannah, event sales manager for the Pensacola Blue Wahoos, said the team is building out logistics now rather than waiting for the official result. Planning for a concert with a few days of lead time is unusual for the stadium, but the organization is treating the event as if it will happen. Blue Wahoos Stadium sits steps from Community Maritime Park, where the outdoor portion of the celebration would be staged.

Reeves is coordinating with Escambia County Commissioner Lumon May on the event details. The concert would be free to the public. The city has also prepared a backup plan to honor Richardson regardless of whether she advances to the Top 3.

Richardson performed Circle of Life from The Lion King on April 20 during the show\'s Disney Night, drawing Jennifer Hudson to tears from the judges\' table. A mural in her honor has already appeared on Graffiti Bridge. The Top 3 will be announced May 4, and the hometown visits — if Richardson is among them — would follow immediately, with filming in Pensacola on May 5.
